speaker: interviewer
text: --25, 1996, Sydney, Australia. Elizabeth, you were telling us about your decision not to go back to the work camp, but to go-- not to go back to the factory, but to go to your father. What did your step-mother say?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was not so happy. But she sees she can't talk to me. So then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she followed me, but we don't talk much. And then I go to-- my father was so upset that I'm there. But when I arrived, about two minutes, again, the bell ring. And that man come again. And he said, they changed their mind. They take the girls away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I went under the bed, to nobody should see me. And when he went, I said to my father, look, if I go back now, they shot me straight away. Because I was a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Escapee

speaker: interviewee
text: Escape. So what you want what I should do? If I go back, that's the end of it. Because the transport is gone. So what they do with me? Shot me. So, then he go to conference again with his wife to the other room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he come back. And he said, you know, stay. We will think something about it. You know, we-- we need time to-- to think about it. And that's what do. That's what we do all afternoon. That was already about 5 o'clock in the afternoon already. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we talk about it. And he said, you know, I have a friend. He has a friend, a school friend, that priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Klobetz, Sandor. They was in the school together. And my father always help him. Because my father was a generous man with everybody. Not with his family, but-- not with me. But to the other people, he was always a gentlemen. And he always help him. And he said, I think him, and I talk to him. So he ring him. And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think about it. Give me a few hours. And then the night come. And I can't sleep in his place, because you never know when they come to see what's happening. So my grandmother, his-- his mother's house was about half a corner from his house. And a non-Jewish girl lived there. It was a beautiful home. And they give it to her. And she looked after it. And she lived there. And they said when they come home, they will see what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father have a key. And they took me there when it was dark. And [INAUDIBLE] to room where is the housekeeper used to sleep. So they put me there. And tomorrow morning, they said I will talk to that [NON-ENGLISH]. The priest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Priest, and then we will see what's coming. And that's how was it. The next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father sent somebody. I don't know, who was it? I think it was his wife who is go to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. How you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Town hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Town hall. And I have a good friend of mine there, a girl who was with me together from the kindergarten. We grew up together. But she don't live that town anymore. She went to [INAUDIBLE] in Czechoslovakia. Her father was an [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officer of the air force. And she went studying in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was not living in Ujpest. So we take her papers out. Because I know when she born, her auntie name, her grandmother. Because we was very close together. So we get the papers. You had no trouble getting the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because they-- they don't know she's not in that town anymore. And they give it to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you applied for it? Or, your stepmother applied?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my step-mother. She went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like a very Jewish name.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's in Hungary. The Bible, Bible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Biblical name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Biblical name. And we was-- how I say to you. Together-- we went to the high school together. We went together. Only when I go to the technical college, she went to-- she come to be a-- not a nurse. It's a-- in the countryside, you, you know, they look after the sick people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't need ID to get the papers out on this girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got all her identification papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birth certificate, everything. I only needed birth certificate. That's-- that's all what I need, because that's the everything. And it was the same age, and how I know where the aunt is, and the grandmother, the grandfather name. I-- I know everybody. And the priest had a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] holiday place, actually, in Balaton. It's a grapes--

speaker: interviewer
text: A vineyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: A vineyard, yeah. And he has a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] housekeeper. And the housekeeper's mother look after his property over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was an elderly woman who lived alone. So she was happy if I go to her and help her to the winery and things and the chickens, and the moo cows, and everything. And I went there. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time in my life-- I never-- never was that side of the country. I go with the train. And when-- when the train is gone, then it's an air things come again.

speaker: interviewer
text: An air raid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Air raid. And they put us out again, because that was the rule. Have to leave the-- the wagon, or the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Carriage, you have to leave. So we're running down to the field. And I was hoping it's coming a moment, killing me, you know? I was so unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know why I live, you know? Only one thing was to see my mother again. But was hopeless, because now-- then I heard that they went in different way. How can I get over there? Because now they push me that side of the-- of the country. And she went that side of country. Did you think she was still alive at the stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because nobody see her coming out of wagon-- of the wagon. They see her going in. And she started to call me and my brother, because she was alone with my uncle and my auntie and my cousin. And-- and she calling my name. But nobody see coming off the train in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you the she called out your name? Did someone tell you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because I was looking for her, you know, to find her. That was-- even if I can't find her, but at least to know where is she-- where is she.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know that she went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in the [NON-ENGLISH] camp with his friend. They-- because they don't look Jewish. My brother was blonde, and curly, and blue eyes, like my grandfather used to, my mother always said. And they escaped. And they find him-- find them. And one of his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friend come back said, front of them, they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they trying to escape? Or, were they just shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- they tried to escape. The tried to escape. And they got-- got them and bring them back. In front of the other boys, they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Firing squad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard this after the war, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. He was only 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, you told me that there's a list of martyr names in the synagogue in Ujpest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the names are there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my name, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody escape in that transport who go from that factory. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one you were supposed to be on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Nobody come home. So the factory give the list to the [INAUDIBLE] people, for those girls are gone. And when was the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. My memory-- the memory. You know, that wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Memorial wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: The memorial wall. And I read my family name. And that was my name there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they thought that you had also been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yes. They don't know I escaped. Nobody knows. They think I was in  the wagon with the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, what happened to your father's family? The Jewish family of your father, were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They all went to Auschwitz. All-- my-- my grandmother was 72 like me now. And I have auntie. And I have a cousin. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your father react when he heard that they were all being taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once he told me-- once, I don't know what he mean by then. But he said to me, if once more your mother coming back, it will be a different life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he mean by that do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he-- he wanted to change his life. Or-- but he was only 48. My mother was 46. And he was 48 when they kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Balatonakali.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Balatonakali. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to live in the priest's summer house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there about till September.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do everything. And by-- by hand, I make some frocks for the village girls. And they bring me some presents. So I have a lovely life, because everybody likes me. And I sing with them. And I milk the cows. Like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived with the housekeeper's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --old mother? And were they still making wine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Yeah, they still have the-- they still have the--

speaker: interviewer
text: But were they making wine when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other workers there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. But they say to them I am the priest's niece. So they give me a covet, because I was somebody. But I still mixed with them, so they-- they think I am so generous, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they never suspect you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they not think it was suspicious that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because, you know, the priest have a little boy cousin who used to be there in the summer holiday. And-- but this year, he went in a camp with the school. So he can't come. So that's why I was there. That's how they understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though there was a war going on, life was normal in the country like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. And the non-Jewish people, they have a ball of their life. Because all the Jewish money, and the house, and the jewelry and everything, they live like queens and kings. You know? It's not their money and not their house. They're just sitting in the beautiful homes to the Jewish people. They was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have news of other Jews at this stage? Other members of your family or friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but when I come-- I asked the priest-- I wanted to go back to the city, you know? And he was a very sick man. He has asthma, a very sick man. And he need always somebody around him to look after him with the medicine. And that's what I do to him when he was with us in that summer place in Balaton. And of course, he has a little chapel over there. And she-- he always pray every morning, make a mass. And I help him. He teach me what to do, how to do. I know everything. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But he knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he wanted you to learn all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because he wants I should become a Catholic, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't saving you as a Jew. He wanted to save you as a Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He don't say. But I have a feeling. Because that's why he teach me, isn't it? If he don't want I should become one of his people, yeah, then he wouldn't do that, yeah? And he loves me-- he liked me. Because I was a clever little girl, and I do everything to please him. And I learn. I learn a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He teach me how to dress them up to go to the mass. And to give them the wine and prepare the things. I was the little boy-- you know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: The alter boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I was alter girl. So-- and every morning, I prayed with him. And-- and-- because maybe-- because my grandfather was a Catholic, how I told you. Maybe I was not a [INAUDIBLE] Jew, you know? I was a between them, you know? Half, half way. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoy it, yes. Because I always like to know everything. And-- and it was something new for me, you know? I never-- I never learned that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was fascinated, you know? And-- and-- because he was so good to me, I'd do anything to him to please him. And I know he like that I should learn that, so I learned for him. So you-- so you helped him in the chapel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yes. And I cleaned the chapel. I put the fresh flowers there, you know? And he was so proud of me. And when-- when he took me back to his place in the city, unfortunately, the-- the man who was working-- to the-- to the bellman, he has a bomb kill him, and was nobody to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. But he teach me in his-- in the vineyard, in the little chapel. So I know everything. So I went every morning and help them to the priests to put on dress and the [INAUDIBLE]. This is when you went back to Rakospalota.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you get back to Rakospalota, also the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And when we-- I come with the-- with the-- I call him uncle, you know. And when we go to the station, then I help him. Because he was so sick and weak, I hold him in his hand and his arm. And then somebody said, stop that Jewish girl. And for a moment, I think they're calling me. And he hold my hand. He said, don't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walk slowly and slowly. And we don't move the head. And next to me, a Jewish girl was there. And somebody recognized her. And they came and take her away. How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine how I feel? I was so shaky. And I don't know what to do. Because-- he said, just calm down, calm down, calm down, you know? And we walk so slowly. I think he help me, not I help him that time. Because I was so upset and so shocked to see the other girl is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there guards on the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everywhere, of course. Everywhere and every-- and they walk around the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Railway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everywhere, of course. And they stop, and-- don't need to-- can I have your papers? Can I have your papers, you know? Everywhere, because was many--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they German guards, or Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Germans and Hungarians everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the worst?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the Hungarians was worst. I think so. Because they go to their head. They think they are somebody now. They-- all [INAUDIBLE] those nobody's who that time was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because if somebody was somebody, they don't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course they were cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they cruel to other Hungarians as well, or just to Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to Jews. And those ones who-- who sympathizing with the Jews, of course. They was rude to them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you see things happen, people being killed, shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- look, they take my father away. And my father was so sure of, always, of himself. So he smacked the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: --guard, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when they take them to shot them in the [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --only-- only only he-- his hand was strangled together and the feet. Because he would escape. And he want to kill everybody. And they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you about this incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife come to me to the priest's house. Because that was already in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your father shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was already after November?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This next day, she come to me and told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they come to his house? What did she tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they know he has all the-- the jewelry things for-- for everybody, for my family, and his family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because only he was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was suspicious for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Yes, he was suspicious. But he don't believe it. He was thinking that nothing will happen to him, because Mr. Jeno Spitzer, you know? Nothing would happen to him. But he was wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? They came?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came 4 o'clock in the morning. And they took him away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do when they came to the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they put everything upside down. And my father told my little brother who was 10 at that time-- my half brother-- he said, in [NON-ENGLISH], in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pantry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pantry. They put the jewelry there. If anything happened to them, he should know. And when they come, the little boy panicked and run straight away to the pantry. And they take everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took all the money and the jewelry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took your father. They left your stepmother and stepbrother? And where did they take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take to the-- to the-- [NON-ENGLISH] house. And then they interview him. And next morning, they take him to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The Danube.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And shot him, 10 of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your stepmother came to tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she want you to say? What did she expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Probably she don't come to me. I think she-- she go to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The priest.

speaker: interviewee
text: To his friend, yes. Because he-- she knows he liked him. They was very close when even was young boys. They grew up together, and they went to school together. And I think she come to him, not me. But I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told you the news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's-- now it's-- everything what is not good then, now it's finished. So I think I-- I am finished. But that uncle, that priest was so nice to me. He said to me, you're my little girl, now. I am, you know, your father's friend. So-- and long as I live, I look after you. I don't let anything happen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your stepmother say anything similar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say to you when you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your father is gone. The killed him. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was not a-- not a-- soft people, you know? She was a-- in my eyes-- maybe, maybe because of me. But probably if my stepbrother heard that-- that one, you know, he will be upset. But, you know, when my brother write me a letter and come to Australia and I help him to come here-- because like my mother teach me, it's not his fault. He's your brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was about 20, 22 at that time. And I said to him, you want your mother to come out after you? He said, yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't like her. I-- I never like her. But I said to my husband, I said, listen darling, that's his mother. My mother teach me to like him, so I think we should try to bring her out.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Australia. And I was sure if he-- if she come, she make miserable my life again, you know, even to see her. Not to live with her, but to see her. But I said to my darling husband. I said, but we have to do it for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we fixed it up the papers and everything. We got a deed. And we sent the ticket to the boat and everything. And then she get a cancer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the day when she should come out, she died. So the God-- I believe God. And I said, she don't-- the God wanted to I should make the sacrifice to show Him what type of person I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bring her out. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he don't let her come to make my life miserable. I believe that. So after she told you the news of your father, did she go back to her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, she was fighting off that-- that property. She take her children from her first marriage to my father's house-- not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll finish. Thanks.
